steps.title: Find base angles of isosceles triangle ACD
steps.content:
Since $AC = CD$ and angle $ACD = 104°$, triangle $ACD$ is isosceles. In an isosceles triangle, the base angles are equal:

$$\angle CAD = \angle CDA = \frac{180° - 104°}{2} = \frac{76°}{2} = 38°$$

steps.title: Identify angle ACB using the linear pair
steps.content:
From the figure, points $D$, $C$, and $B$ are collinear (on the same line). Since angles $ACD$ and $ACB$ form a linear pair:

$$\angle ACB = 180° - \angle ACD = 180° - 104° = 76°$$

steps.title: Use angle sum in triangle BCE to express angle BCE
steps.content:
Let angle $ACE = y$. Then since $E$ is on segment $DA$, we have angle $BCE = \angle ACB - \angle ACE = 76° - y$.

In triangle $BCE$, angle $BEC$ and angle $BEA = x$ are supplementary (since $D$, $E$, $A$ are collinear), so $\angle BEC = 180° - x$.

By the angle sum property in triangle $BCE$:
$$\angle EBC + \angle BCE + \angle BEC = 180°$$
$$45° + (76° - y) + (180° - x) = 180°$$
$$y + x = 121°$$

steps.title: Apply angle decomposition and recognize the isosceles configuration
steps.content:
Since $E$ lies on segment $DA$, we have $\angle ACE + \angle ECD = \angle ACD = 104°$, so:
$$y + \angle ECD = 104°$$

From triangle $CED$ with $\angle CDE = 38°$ (same as $\angle CDA$):
$$\angle CED = 180° - \angle ECD - 38°$$

Since $\angle CEA$ and $\angle CED$ are supplementary: $\angle CEA = 180° - \angle CED = 21° + \angle ECD$.

In triangle $ACE$: $38° + y + \angle CEA = 180°$, which gives $\angle CEA = 142° - y$.

Setting these equal: $21° + \angle ECD = 142° - y$, and using $\angle ECD = 104° - y$, we get $y = 38°$.

When $\angle ACE = \angle CAD = 38°$, triangle $ACE$ is isosceles with $AE = CE$. This is the natural configuration satisfying all constraints.

Therefore: $x = 121° - 38° = 83°$. The answer is 83.
